comments.body: The code won't work anyway as it doesn't maintain add(i) in the sorted order as expected by loop using set(ctr, i). eg try List.of(1,2,6,7,8,9).stream()

Type arguments have to be placed after the dot, not before it. It don’t know why nobody was able to name that simple syntax error. However, there is rarely a need for that in practice, as you’d use the collector definition in a context where the types are implied. You could even use Collector.of(ArrayList::new, (ArrayList<Integer> acc, Integer i ) -> { /* … */ }, (a,b)-> { a.addAll(b); return a;}); to get a collector with the correct stand-alone type.
I was actually looking for way to actually pass type parameters to of method then how can I do that ?

answers.body:
You specify the Type after the dot, not before. Like so:

Collector.<Integer, ArrayList<Integer>, ArrayList<Integer>>of(ArrayList::new, (  acc,  i ) -> {
            if ( acc.size() > 4 )
            {
                for ( int ctr = acc.size() - 1; ctr >= 0; ctr-- )
                {
                    if ( i > acc.get(ctr) )
                    {
                        acc.set(ctr, i);
                        break;
                    }
                }
            }
            else
            {
                acc.add(i);
            }
        }, ( a,  b)-> { a.addAll(b); return a;}, Function.identity(), Collector.Characteristics.IDENTITY_FINISH);

Alternatively, you could specify the Lambda's Typing and the rest would be inferred as well, or assign it to a typed variable before using it.

title: How to pass type parameter explicity to Collector.of method
body:
I was trying to solve Java stream practise problem where I have to find out largest 5 Integers from given finite stream of Integer. I came up with below solution.


    new Random().ints(200, 1, 200000).boxed().collect(Collector.of(ArrayList<Integer>::new, ( acc, i ) -> {
        if ( acc.size() > 4 )
        {
            for ( int ctr = acc.size() - 1; ctr >= 0; ctr-- )
            {
                if ( i > acc.get(ctr) )
                {
                    acc.set(ctr, i);
                    break;
                }
            }
        }
        else
        {
            acc.add(i);
        }
    }, (a,b)-> { a.addAll(b); return a;}, Function.identity(), Collector.Characteristics.IDENTITY_FINISH))
    .forEach(System.out::println);;

My question is if I have to pass type parameter of Collector explicitly to Collector.of method then how to do that ? Below code gives error

Collector<Integer, ArrayList<Integer>, ArrayList<Integer>>.of(ArrayList<Integer>::new, ( acc, i ) -> {
            if ( acc.size() > 4 )
            {
                for ( int ctr = acc.size() - 1; ctr >= 0; ctr-- )
                {
                    if ( i > acc.get(ctr) )
                    {
                        acc.set(ctr, i);
                        break;
                    }
                }
            }
            else
            {
                acc.add(i);
            }
        }, (a,b)-> { a.addAll(b); return a;}, Function.identity(), Collector.Characteristics.IDENTITY_FINISH)

Errors are

  • ArrayList cannot be resolved to a variable
    • Collector cannot be resolved to a variable
    • Integer cannot be resolved to a variable
    • The method of(ArrayList::new, ( acc, i) -> {}, ( a, b) -> {}, Function.identity(), Collector.Characteristics) is undefined for the type StreamPractise
    • Syntax error on token ",", delete this token
    • Integer cannot be resolved to a variable
    • Integer cannot be resolved to a variable
    • Syntax error on token ".", delete this token
    • ArrayList cannot be resolved to a variable
